title: The Monthly Crucifixion, St. Macarius of Egypt, St. Matrona of Barcelona & St. John of Lycopolis
narrative:
title": "The Monthly Crucifixion, St. Macarius of Egypt, St. Matrona of Barcelona & St. John of Lycopolis

narrative": "The Monthly Commemoration of the Crucifixion, St. Macarius of Egypt, St. Matrona of Barcelona, and St. John of Lycopolis

On the twenty-seventh of Megabit, the holy Church observes the monthly commemoration of the Crucifixion — that recurring Friday-of-the-month on which the Ethiopian liturgical tradition returns to the foot of the Cross to renew the community's awareness that the Lenten fast is not an arbitrary religious exercise but an entry into the specific suffering of the one who fasted forty days in the desert and was then handed over to be crucified. The monthly commemoration of the Cross in the midst of the Great Fast has a particular intensity that the other monthly observances do not quite match: the community is already in the posture of the fast, already emptied and waiting, and the return to the Cross at this moment of deliberate deprivation allows the faithful to feel in their weakened bodies something of the solidarity with the one who suffered that the fast is designed to create. The Cross this month is not a symbol to be contemplated from a comfortable distance but a reality to be entered by a community already standing in its shadow.

Saint Macarius of Egypt appears for the third time in the Megabit cycle, each appearance illuminating a different dimension of this incomparable father: the healer, the teacher of prayer, and now the penitent. The tradition preserves a story about Macarius that the Lenten commemoration of the Crucifixion makes most transparent: that he once accidentally killed a gadfly that was biting him, and then spent six months in the harshest part of the Egyptian desert as a penance, exposing himself to the bites of every insect that came to him, until his body was so disfigured that his disciples recognised him only by his voice when he returned. The tradition does not commend this particular penance as a model but preserves it as a sign of Macarius's absolute seriousness about the interior life — that he treated the smallest act of involuntary violence as requiring a response, because in his economy of prayer there was no action too small to matter.

Saint Matrona of Barcelona is this day's discovery from the western Mediterranean: a slave woman of that city who was a Christian and who was killed by her Jewish master when he discovered that she had been attending Christian liturgies. Her martyrdom is one of the rare early cases in the Latin martyrological tradition that involves a Jewish perpetrator rather than a Roman one, and the tradition preserves it without anti-Jewish polemicism, noting simply that her master killed her for her faith and that she died for it. The Ethiopian tradition honours Matrona as a witness to the truth that the martyrological vocation is not the property of the free and powerful: the slave woman who died for attending the Eucharist is as fully a martyr as the general who died for refusing the imperial sacrifice.

Saint John of Lycopolis in the Thebaid is the great prophetic hermit of the fourth century: a monk who sealed himself in three cells carved into the rock of a mountain near Lycopolis (modern Asyut in Egypt) and who for forty years received visitors only through a small window on Sundays and Saturdays, speaking with them and answering their questions but never leaving his enclosure. He is known particularly for a series of prophetic messages delivered to the Emperor Theodosius before his military campaigns, which the tradition records came true with precision. The Cross monthly renewed, Macarius weeping for a fly, Matrona killed in Barcelona for attending the Eucharist, John speaking through a window to the emperor: four images of the love that holds nothing back. Amen.
